exemplar.title: A mathematical approach and exploration of expected value of score in darts game

detailedExemplarData.title: A mathematical approach and exploration of expected value of score in darts game

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear logical sequence with introduction, methodology, models and conclusion
Consistent use of headings and wellâplaced diagrams and tables
Professional title page and wellâstructured Table 1
weaknesses: Inconsistent page numbering and figure labels
Some lengthy algebraic derivations add unnecessary bulk
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place coherence and organization at band 3 and conciseness at band 2, indicating a wellâorganized exploration that is mostly concise. The work maintains clear structure and logical development but falls short of exceptional brevity and polish.

strengths: Mostly consistent and correct mathematical notation throughout
Effective use of multiple representations (equations, tables, charts, sketches)
Good logical structure linking definitions, calculations and results
weaknesses: Occasional notation slips (missing absoluteâvalue bars, cmÂ²âmmÂ² inconsistencies)
Fragmented algebra across lines impedes clarity at points
Informal phrases (âand so onâ) replace formal summation notation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands were assessed at band 3 (âmostly consistentâ, âeffectiveâ, âgood clarityâ). Minor notation and clarity issues prevent a topâband score, but overall communication is strong.

strengths: Choice and design of two distinct accuracy models show independent thinking
Personal narrative of injury and selfâassessment motivates the investigation
Good initiative in generating visualisations and verifying pdf conditions
weaknesses: Critique of modeling assumptions is limited
No primary data collection or exploration of alternative targets
Creativity is solid but not exceptional
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strands fall in band 2 for independent thinking, personal approach and creativity. The student demonstrates clear personal engagement but does not reach the outstanding level demanded for band 3.

weaknesses: Limited depth of reflection and critical analysis
No quantitative comparison between models and data
Evaluation of outcomes remains basic without discussing realâworld biases
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All reflection strands are in band 1 (âlimitedâ reflection, âbasicâ analysis, âlimitedâ evaluation). The student lists limitations but does not engage in substantial or deep critical reflection.

strengths: Use of concentricâring geometry, summations and Jacobian transformations is appropriate
Content is at course level with double integrals and normal pdfs
Correct verification of pdf integration and Jacobian derivations
weaknesses: Minor numerical inconsistencies and rounding errors
Probability table sums to less than 1 indicating an error
Mistake in normal pdf exponent denominator
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work applies relevant mathematics appropriately (band 4) and demonstrates courseâlevel understanding (band 4) but has minor errors in accuracy (band 3). An overall score of 3 reflects good understanding with some mistakes preventing a higher mark.

strandSelections.reasoning: Arguments are generally clear: definitions â area calculations â probabilities â expected value.  Paragraphing and transitions support the logic, though occasional algebra is fragmented across lines, so clarity is âgoodâ rather than âexcellentâ.

Area computations and probability weights are mostly correct and coherent, but minor numerical inconsistencies (e.g., Bull area rounding, typo in table headings) and an imprecise switch from mmÂ² to cmÂ² reveal understanding that is âgood with minor errorsâ rather than thoroughly correct.

The student employs equations, tables, bar charts, radial sketches and Desmos/Wolfram graphs, each helping to illustrate results.  Representations link well to the text (e.g., Table 2 leads directly to Diagram 4), satisfying âeffectiveâ use.

The exploration follows a clear sequence (introduction â methodology â models â conclusion) and each section builds on the previous one, so the logical thread is easy to follow.  Minor digressions (e.g., lengthy quotations of integration theory) momentarily interrupt the flow, preventing the work from reaching the âexcellentâ descriptor, but overall coherence is maintained.

Designing planar and normal models, generating Desmos visualisations and checking the Jacobian transformation display a âgood levelâ of initiative, yet no primary data collection or optimisation of alternative targets is attempted, limiting creativity to mid-band.

Some basic analysis is offered (e.g., noting equal-area models overestimate weak-player scores), yet no quantitative comparison with data or alternative strategies is performed, so the strand sits in the âbasic analysisâ band.
The student lists strengths and limitations and suggests future experiments, but does not test or refine the model, and therefore the evaluation remains âlimitedâ.

Reflection is present but brief: the student states that the normal model âbetter reflects realityâ without probing why deviations occur or how parameters could be calibrated.  This constitutes âlimited reflectionâ.

Variables are defined, summations and integral notation are appropriate, and vectors are typeset correctly.  Occasional slips (e.g., missing absolute-value bars, inconsistent use of cmÂ² vs mmÂ²) appear but do not impede understanding, so language is âmostly consistentâ rather than fully âaccurate throughoutâ.

The investigation is motivated by the studentâs injury and self-assessment aims; this narrative is woven into the introduction and conclusion.  The work remains mainly mathematical rather than autobiographical, meeting the descriptor for a âclear personal approachâ.

Most content is relevant to the research question, yet long algebraic derivations of the 2-D normal integral and repeated explanations of basic dart rules add bulk without advancing the investigation.  This places conciseness as âmostly relevant with some unnecessary elementsâ.

Geometry of concentric rings, expected-value summations, continuous radial probability densities and Jacobian transformations are relevant to the aim and applied appropriately in two distinct models, demonstrating effective (though not comprehensive) use of mathematics.

The choice to design two accuracy models and to verify the pdf conditions shows personal reasoning beyond class examples.  However, independent critique of assumptions is modest and largely confined to a short âlimitationsâ list, so the evidence is âsignificantâ but not âoutstandingâ.

Headings and sub-headings are consistently used, diagrams and tables are embedded close to the text they support, and the appendix is sign-posted.  There is a recognizable introduction, body and conclusion, though page numbering and some figure labels are inconsistent, so the structure is âclearâ rather than âexceptionalâ.

Content is solidly at HL level: double integrals, variable transformations and normal-distribution pdfs.  Some steps rely on external CAS output rather than student derivation, preventing a move into the top-two bands.

annotations.comment: The title page is professionally presented, with a clear course and task identification that immediately orients the reader.
Including Diagram 6 to visualize the planar distribution helps readers see how probability density is uniform across the circle.
Including the page count on the title page adds little to the mathematical content; this administrative detail could be removed or moved to an administrative cover sheet.
The personal narrative linking the injury to the choice of darts demonstrates genuine engagement and motivation beyond the classroom.
The research goal is described broadly; consider stating a precise research question (e.g. âHow does throw precision Ï affect expected score?â).
Diagram 1 effectively illustrates the board layout, aiding comprehension of subsequent geometrical reasoning.
The source of board dimensions is cited in a footnote; consider moving the reference into a formal bibliography for consistency.
The assumption of aiming at the center is logically justified, simplifying the model and reflecting a weak playerâs optimal strategy.
The student should reflect more deeply on how this central-aiming assumption might bias results, especially for skilled players targeting T20.
The use of $$\frac{1+2+\dots+20}{20}=10.5$$ to find the mean sector value is correct and clearly presented.
In the text you refer to multiplying by 2 and 3, but you omit explicitly stating $$10.5\times2=21$$ and $$10.5\times3=31.5$$; include these to avoid ambiguity.
The conversion from mmÂ² to cmÂ² is performed without clarifying the factor of 100; this unit change should be shown explicitly.
Consider showing the full intermediate calculation for $$A_{inner}$$ and $$A_{outer}$$ to strengthen the demonstration of area derivations.
Table 1 is well structured: variables, formulae and numerical values are aligned, making it easy to reference later.
There are small rounding inconsistencies (e.g. inner bull 1.27 vs expected 1.27â¦); double-check significant figures against board specifications.
Table 2âs probabilities should sum to 1; the current specific-region probabilities sum to less than 1, indicating a calculation error.
Reflect on how these theoretical probabilities compare to actual throwing data; this would deepen your evaluation of the geometric model.
The final expected-value calculation uses 38.23 instead of the previously derived 8.23 for $$A_{single}$$, a discrepancy that must be corrected.
Break the long expression for $$Es(Z)$$ into smaller parts and annotate each term to guide the reader through its composition.
The cylinder analogy in the planar model is over-simplistic; discuss limitations of mapping a 2D board to a 3D volume.
The piecewise definition of $$E(r)$$ is technically correct but would benefit from clearer sub-case headings for each radius interval.
Label the axes of Diagram 7 explicitly (e.g. âradius r (mm)â and âExpected scoreâ) to enhance clarity.
The formula for $$f(x,y)$$ incorrectly places $$2\pi\sigma^2$$ in the exponentâs denominator; it should be $$2\sigma^2$$.
The description of domains D and Î is minimal; include a diagram or verbal description to link the regions in the double integral.
The verification that the normal pdf integrates to $$\pi$$ in polar form is correct, showing mastery of advanced integration.
Although correct, the text does not explicitly state that $$\lim_{R\to\infty}(1-e^{-R^2})=1$$; adding this comment would complete the argument.
Confirming the Jacobian for the $u,v$ substitution and showing $2\pi\sigma^2$ demonstrates a strong understanding of multivariable transformations.
The use of âand so onâ in the expected-value summation is informal; replace with summation notation $$\sum_{i=1}^{n}$$ for clarity.
The ââStrengths of Investigationââ section links mathematical progression to practical applications, reflecting a good personal approach.
The limitations list acknowledges key issues but does not discuss measurement errors or skill variation; expand to include these real-world factors.
Reflect on how varying $$\sigma$$ affects the bell curveâs spread and thus the expected score, linking parameter to performance.
The Jacobian derivation transitioning from Cartesian to polar coordinates is concise and accurate, demonstrating strong calculus skills.
The conclusion summarizes findings but lacks critical evaluation of model limitations and potential biases.
Consider including quantitative comparison (e.g. error percentages) between geometric and normal models to strengthen the evaluation.
